back: A relation is in 1NF if it contains only atomic (indivisible) values. Each column must contain a single value, and each row must be unique. No repeating groups or arrays allowed.
A relation is in 2NF if it's in 1NF and all non-key attributes are fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key. No partial dependency allowed.
A relation is in 3NF if it's in 2NF and has no transitive dependencies. Non-key attributes must depend only on the primary key, not on other non-key attributes.
BCNF is a stricter version of 3NF. For every functional dependency X → Y, X must be a superkey. Every determinant must be a candidate key.
A partial dependency exists when a non-key attribute is functionally dependent on part of a composite primary key. This violates 2NF.
A transitive dependency occurs when a non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute, which in turn depends on the primary key. This violates 3NF.
A functional dependency (X → Y) exists when the value of X uniquely determines the value of Y. For each value of X, there is exactly one value of Y.
A candidate key is a minimal superkey that can uniquely identify each tuple. A relation can have multiple candidate keys, one of which is chosen as the primary key.
A superkey is a set of one or more attributes that can uniquely identify tuples in a relation. It contains a candidate key.
Decomposition is the process of splitting a relation into two or more smaller relations to eliminate anomalies and achieve higher normal forms.
A decomposition is lossless-join if natural join of the decomposed relations yields the original relation without spurious tuples.
Dependency preservation ensures that all functional dependencies from the original relation can be enforced using the decomposed relations without needing to join them.

front: A prime attribute is an attribute that is part of any candidate key of a relation. Non-prime attributes are not part of any candidate key.
What are insertion, deletion, and update anomalies?
back: Insertion anomaly: Cannot add data due to missing attributes. Deletion anomaly: Losing data when deleting related data. Update anomaly: Inconsistent data due to redundant storage.
